# The Bayesian spatial coverage model {#sec-model}

@sec-inputs left us with a fixed accessibility operator, calibrated
distributions for demand and capacity, and a specification frozen and hashed
before any result was seen. This chapter fits the one model that sits between
those inputs and the desert probabilities: an intercept-only Besag–York–Mollié
(BYM2) model that smooths tract coverage across the map. The work is a single
call to `fit_coverage_bym2()` in `R/fct_bym2.R`, orchestrated by
`scripts/02-1_fit_bym2.R`. It runs on the full-pipeline track only.

## Why a spatial model

Tract-level coverage is a noisy quantity. A tract with few children and one
nearby provider can post an extreme E2SFCA score for reasons that are as much
sampling accident as structural fact, and adjacent tracts — which share roads,
providers, and demand — carry information about each other that a tract-by-tract
reading throws away. A spatial model uses that information. It **borrows
strength** across neighbors, pulling each tract's estimate partway toward its
surroundings by an amount the data itself chooses.

The BYM2 model does this while keeping two kinds of variation apart
[@besag_bayesian_1991; @riebler_intuitive_2016]. Some variation is
**structured** — smooth patterns shared by neighboring tracts, the signature of
real regional differences in access. Some is **unstructured** — tract-specific
noise with no spatial pattern. Separating them matters because only the
structured part should be borrowed across the map; treating noise as signal would
smear it into neighbors. The model estimates how much of the total variation is
of each kind, rather than assuming it.

## The response and the likelihood

The response is the accessible-slot allocation the fixed operator sends to each
tract under the survey-calibrated *expected* capacity, and the exposure is the
ACS demand estimate. With a log link, a log-demand offset, and an intercept-only
mean, the model is

$$
\log \mu_t = \log \hat D_t + \alpha + b_t ,
$$

so that $\mu_t / \hat D_t = \exp(\alpha + b_t)$ is the modeled coverage **rate**:
the offset turns a model of allocated slots into a model of slots per child, and
the spatial term $b_t$ does the smoothing. In `scripts/02-1_fit_bym2.R` the
response and exposure are formed directly from the @sec-inputs objects:

```r
expected_capacity <- providers$licensed_capacity * providers$sector_mean_multiplier
expected_rate_all <- as.numeric(supply_bundle$operator %*% expected_capacity)
exposure <- demand$demand_mean[eligible]     # ACS point estimate D-hat
response <- expected_rate_all[eligible] * exposure
```

The likelihood is a **Tweedie** compound Poisson–gamma,
$Y_t \sim \mathrm{Tw}(\mu_t, \xi, \psi)$, with power parameter $1 < \xi < 2$
[@jorgensen_exponential_1987]. This is a **registered deviation** from the
planned Poisson likelihood, and the reason is that the response is continuous
with exact zeros, not a count: **1,206** of the 1,409 tract responses are
noninteger, and **203** are exact zeros (the structural zeros from @sec-inputs —
tracts with no reachable capacity). A Tweedie with a power between one and two is
built for exactly that shape — a positive continuous part with a spike of exact
zeros — and it keeps the log-demand offset intact. The obvious workaround,
rounding the allocations to integer counts so a Poisson would accept them, was
**rejected**: it would alter the data to fit the model rather than choosing a
model that fits the data. Under a full refit (Track A), the posterior means of
the Tweedie parameters land near $\xi \approx 1.48$ (power) and $\psi \approx 3.56$
(dispersion); these are diagnostics of the fitted model object rather than values
in the shipped registry.

This study departs from its preregistered plan in exactly two places, and it
treats both as registered deviations — documented and motivated rather than
quietly applied. The first, the one-child demand floor, we met in @sec-inputs.
The second is this likelihood family. `scripts/02-1_fit_bym2.R` records it — the
planned family, what was implemented, the trigger, the rejected workaround, and
the scientific reason — to `outputs/tables/02_model_deviation_register.csv`.

## The BYM2 spatial prior

BYM2 is a reparameterization of the original Besag–York–Mollié model that makes
its two components interpretable and gives them priors on a sensible scale
[@riebler_intuitive_2016]. The spatial term is

$$
b = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\tau}}\left(\sqrt{1-\phi}\,v + \sqrt{\phi}\,u^{*}\right),
$$

where $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ is the **unstructured** component, $u^{*}$ is a
**scaled intrinsic conditional autoregressive** (CAR) field — the structured,
spatially smooth component — and $\tau$ is a precision setting the overall
magnitude. The single mixing parameter $\phi \in [0,1]$ is the fraction of
variance that is spatially structured: $\phi$ near zero means the map is mostly
noise, $\phi$ near one means it is mostly smooth regional pattern. Scaling $u^{*}$
to unit generalized variance is what makes $\phi$ and $\tau$ comparable across
different graphs [@riebler_intuitive_2016].

We give the hyperparameters penalized-complexity (PC) priors, which shrink toward
a simpler base model unless the data pulls away [@simpson_penalising_2017]. Two
statements fix them: $P(\sigma > 1) = 0.01$ places a mild ceiling on the total
standard deviation, and $P(\phi < 0.5) = 0.5$ is deliberately neutral about
whether variation is structured or not.

The neighborhood structure is a Queen-contiguity graph — tracts are neighbors if
they share any boundary point — over the 1,409 eligible tracts. It has **8,418**
directed links, a mean of **5.97** neighbors per tract, **1** island (a tract
with no neighbors), and **2** connected components. `prepare_p07_graph()` builds
it by subsetting the full graph audited at Step 0.2 down to the eligible tracts:

```r
model_nb <- spdep::subset.nb(full_nb, subset = eligible)   # 1,436 -> 1,409
graph <- INLA::inla.read.graph(Matrix::Matrix(
  spdep::nb2mat(model_nb, style = "B", zero.policy = TRUE), sparse = TRUE
))
```

Because the graph has more than one component, the CAR scaling and the
sum-to-zero identifiability constraint are applied **per connected component**,
which the model formula requests explicitly with `scale.model = TRUE`,
`adjust.for.con.comp = TRUE`, and `constr = TRUE`:

```r
formula <- y ~ 1 + offset(log(exposure)) + f(
  idx,
  model = "bym2",
  graph = graph_info$inla_graph,
  scale.model = TRUE,          # scale the CAR to unit generalized variance
  adjust.for.con.comp = TRUE,  # scale and constrain per connected component
  constr = TRUE,               # sum-to-zero on the structured field
  hyper = list(
    prec = list(prior = "pc.prec", param = c(1, 0.01)),  # P(sigma > 1) = 0.01
    phi  = list(prior = "pc",      param = c(0.5, 0.5))  # P(phi < 0.5) = 0.5
  )
)
```

## Fitting with INLA

We fit with integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA), which computes
accurate posterior marginals for latent Gaussian models like this one without
Markov chain Monte Carlo [@rue_approximate_2009; @lindgren_bayesian_2015]. The
results in the paper use **INLA 26.6.8** with an adaptive Gaussian
approximation strategy, central-composite-design (CCD) integration over the
hyperparameters, and METIS matrix reordering.

INLA is fast, but out of the box it is not bit-reproducible, and for a
replication package that is a problem worth solving carefully. The reason is that
INLA does its heavy numerical work in an **external compiled binary** that can
inherit multithreaded BLAS and OpenMP execution. When several threads reduce a
sum of floating-point numbers, the order in which they finish is not fixed, and
floating-point addition is not associative — so two runs on the same data can
differ in the last digits, and those differences can nudge which hyperparameter
integration configuration the algorithm selects. The fit therefore enforces a
strict **determinism contract**: pin every threading environment variable to one,
run INLA single-threaded (`num.threads = "1:1"`), and set the seed before the
call.

```r
Sys.setenv(
  OMP_NUM_THREADS = "1", OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS = "1", MKL_NUM_THREADS = "1",
  VECLIB_MAXIMUM_THREADS = "1", NUMEXPR_NUM_THREADS = "1"
)
INLA::inla.setOption(num.threads = "1:1", blas.num.threads = 1L)
set.seed(20260716L)

fit <- INLA::inla(
  formula, family = "tweedie", data = model_data,
  control.predictor = list(compute = TRUE),
  control.compute   = list(config = TRUE, dic = TRUE, waic = TRUE, cpo = TRUE),
  control.inla      = list(strategy = "adaptive", int.strategy = "ccd",
                           stupid.search = FALSE, reordering = "metis"),
  num.threads = "1:1", verbose = FALSE
)
```

Serial execution trades a little speed for exact reproducibility: on frozen
inputs the fit returns the same numbers every time, and a refit under a different
linear-algebra backend reproduces them up to negligible drift. @sec-reproducibility
lays out the full seed ledger and the manifest that checks it.

## Diagnostics

The posterior hyperparameters tell a clear story. The total standard deviation is
$\sigma = 0.976$, the precision is $\tau = 1.059$, and the mixing parameter is
$\phi = 0.992$ — meaning the coverage surface is **almost entirely spatially
structured**. Alabama's access geography is smooth regional pattern, not
tract-level noise, which is precisely the setting in which borrowing strength
helps.

One subtlety is worth pausing on, because the code goes out of its way to get it
right. The posterior mean of $\sigma$ is **not** the inverse square root of the
posterior mean precision — that would confuse $\mathbb{E}[1/\sqrt{\tau}]$ with
$1/\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\tau]}$, which are different because the transform is
nonlinear. `extract_bym2_hyperparameters()` instead transforms the whole
precision marginal and takes the expectation on the correct scale:

```r
sigma_marginal <- INLA::inla.tmarginal(function(x) 1 / sqrt(x), precision_marginal)
sigma_mean     <- INLA::inla.emarginal(function(x) x, sigma_marginal)
```

The fit is sound by the usual checks. The posterior fitted coverage tracks the
input coverage closely — Spearman rank correlation **0.934** — confirming the
smoother reorders very few tracts. Model-comparison scores are DIC
**13,317.9** and WAIC **13,262.6**, and there are **0** conditional predictive
ordinate (CPO) failures, so no observation is left unexplained by its own
predictive distribution. These land in `outputs/tables/02_bym2_hyperparameters.csv`
and `outputs/tables/02_bym2_diagnostics.csv`, and the likelihood deviation is
logged in `outputs/tables/02_model_deviation_register.csv`.

## What this produces

The fitted model is saved as one analytic object under the git-ignored `data/`
tree, `data/analytic/02_bym2_fit.rds`. It bundles the INLA fit itself, the
per-tract model data (GEOID, response, exposure, input rate, and posterior fitted
rate), the graph audit, the priors, the likelihood record, the extracted
hyperparameters and diagnostics, and a SHA-256 ledger of every input it consumed
— including the preregistration hash re-checked before the fit. The three
human-readable tables ship under `outputs/tables/`
(`02_bym2_hyperparameters.csv`, `02_bym2_diagnostics.csv`,
`02_model_deviation_register.csv`), and the key posterior quantities are appended
to `outputs/key_numbers.csv`.

This is a single fit — one latent Gaussian field, conditional on the input point
estimates. It is not yet the answer. @sec-posterior takes this fit and draws from
it jointly with the demand and capacity distributions of @sec-inputs, propagating
all three sources of uncertainty at once into the per-tract desert probabilities
that the declaration stage in @sec-declarations then acts on.
